{"body":"[this issue is for not discussion of CHEBI roles. It pertains entirely to the usage of the term "hormone"]

We have:

```
id: GO:0046879
name: hormone secretion
namespace: biological_process
def: "The regulated release of hormones, substances with a specific regulatory effect on a particular organ or group of cells." [ISBN:0198506732]
is_a: GO:0008150 ! biological_process
is_a: GO:0009914 ! hormone transport
is_a: GO:0023061 ! signal release
```

There is only one subclass that indicates mode of dispersal:

```
id: GO:0060986
name: endocrine hormone secretion
namespace: biological_process
def: "The regulated release of a hormone into the circulatory system." 
is_a: GO:0046879 ! hormone secretion
relationship: part_of GO:0050886 ! endocrine process
```

There is no subclasses for autocrine, paracrine, etc.

I think GO:0060986 is fake precision. I think people read "hormone secretion" as being endocrine. 

If you query with GO:0060986 then you miss insulin secretion! There are only 87 mouse annotations to GO:0060986, as opposed to GO:0046879 for hormone signaling. From this we would conclude that endocrine hormone signaling was a rarer form of hormone signaling.

Also, not sure GO:0060986 def is correct. The def includes exocrine system. I think it intends to say releases *directly* into ducts.

Do we really need this level of precision at all? Mechanistically is there a difference in the gene product action between endocrine/exocrine/paracrine/autocrine?

If not, then we can simplify and merge

If there is, then we will have to do work to (a) properly clarify that the parent term encompasses different subtypes and include a do_not_annotate (since you always know the subtype) (b) introduce children not just for endocrine (c) reannotate so we do not have false precision and unnevenness

i haven't looked at the other usages of hormone

> id: GO:0060986
> name: endocrine hormone secretion
> namespace: biological_process
> def: "The regulated release of a hormone into the circulatory system." 

The problem may be at the GO end, with the second definition. Why does a hormone need to travel via a structure recognized by anatomists as a circulatory system in order to qualify as "endocrine"? Relax that constraint to something like "extracellular space" and then perhaps we can use additional criteria like "acting on cells other than the ones that secrete the hormone" or "capable of circulating" or "stable" to distinguish an endocrine one from a paracrine one (acts on the secreting cell or neighboring ones, often because the hormone is unstable (eicosanoids)) and an autocrine one (acts on the cell that produces it, for whatever reason). These distinctions suggest, correctly, that a hormone could have both paracrine and autocrine modes of action (eicosanoids again).

If we can generalize from the mammalian insulin and glucagon cases (no clue whether this is legitimate), an endocrine hormone also has the property that it does not act on the cell that produces it. If that is general, it is a really clean differentium.

The problem may be at the GO end, with the second definition. Why does a hormone need to travel via a structure recognized by anatomists as a circulatory system in order to qualify as "endocrine"? Relax that constraint to something like "extracellular space" and then perhaps we can use additional criteria like "acting on cells other than the ones that secrete the hormone" or "capable of circulating" or "stable" to distinguish an endocrine one from a paracrine one (acts on the secreting cell or neighboring ones, often because the hormone is unstable (eicosanoids)) and an autocrine one (acts on the cell that produces it, for whatever reason). These distinctions suggest, correctly, that a hormone could have both paracrine and autocrine modes of action (eicosanoids again).

If we can generalize from the mammalian insulin and glucagon cases (no clue whether this is legitimate), an endocrine hormone also has the property that it does not act on the cell that produces it. If that is general, it is a really clean differentium.
